subject: Physics
q: A solid sphere, of radius R acquires a terminal velocity <math><msub><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> when falling (due to gravity) through a viscous fluid having a coefficient of viscosity <math><mi>η</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The sphere is broken into 27 identical solid spheres. If each of these spheres acquires a terminal velocity, <math><msub><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mo>,</mo></math> when falling through the same fluid, the ratio <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mfrac><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></mrow><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math> equals:
question: A solid sphere, of radius R acquires a terminal velocity <math><msub><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> when falling (due to gravity) through a viscous fluid having a coefficient of viscosity <math><mi>η</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The sphere is broken into 27 identical solid spheres. If each of these spheres acquires a terminal velocity, <math><msub><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mo>,</mo></math> when falling through the same fluid, the ratio <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mfrac><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></mrow><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math> equals:
solution:
If R is the radius of initial drop and r is the radius of new drop, then
43πR3=27×43πr3⇒r=R3
Terminal velocity,

         vT∝r2
⇒ v1v2=R2R32=9

q:
Two narrow bores of diameter 5.0 mm and 8.0 mm are joined together to form a U-shaped tube open at both ends. If this U-tube contains water, what is the difference in the level of two limbs of the tube.
[Take surface tension of water T=7.3×10-2 N m-1, angle of contact =0, g=10 m s-2 and density of water=1.0×103 kg m-3
question:
Two narrow bores of diameter 5.0 mm and 8.0 mm are joined together to form a U-shaped tube open at both ends. If this U-tube contains water, what is the difference in the level of two limbs of the tube.
[Take surface tension of water T=7.3×10-2 N m-1, angle of contact =0, g=10 m s-2 and density of water=1.0×103 kg m-3
solution:
P0-2TR1+h1ρg=P0-2TR2+h2ρg
h1-h2ρg=2T12.5×10-3-14×10-3⇒h1-h2=2.19 mm

subject: Physics
q: In a test experiment on a model aeroplane in wind tunnel, the flow speeds on the upper and lower surfaces of the wings are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>70</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>65</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> respectively. If the wing area is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>, the lift of the wing is _______<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>. (Given density of air <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
question: In a test experiment on a model aeroplane in wind tunnel, the flow speeds on the upper and lower surfaces of the wings are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>70</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>65</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> respectively. If the wing area is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>, the lift of the wing is _______<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>. (Given density of air <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
solution:
Applying Bernoulli's principle on upper & lower surfaces, we get

P1+12ρv12+ρgh1=P2+12ρv22+ρgh2

Since, height difference between upper and lower surface is negligible.

⇒P2-P1=12ρv12-12ρv22

Therefore, upthrust acting due to pressure difference:

⇒FA=12ρv12-v22⇒F=12×1.2×702-652×2

=810 N

subject: Physics
q: The surface tension of a soap solution is $3.5 \times 10^{-2}$ N/m. The work required to increase the radius of a soap bubble from $1$ cm to $2$ cm is $\alpha \times 10^{-6}$ J. The value of $\alpha$ is _____. ($\pi = 22/7$)
question: The surface tension of a soap solution is $3.5 \times 10^{-2}$ N/m. The work required to increase the radius of a soap bubble from $1$ cm to $2$ cm is $\alpha \times 10^{-6}$ J. The value of $\alpha$ is _____. ($\pi = 22/7$)
solution:
The work done to increase the radius of a soap bubble is given by $W = T \times \Delta A$

Since a soap bubble has two free surfaces, the change in surface area is $\Delta A = 2 \times 4\pi (r_2^2 - r_1^2)$

Substituting the given values:

$W = 3.5 \times 10^{-2} \times 8\pi \times ((2 \times 10^{-2})^2 - (1 \times 10^{-2})^2)$

$W = 3.5 \times 10^{-2} \times 8\pi \times (4 \times 10^{-4} - 1 \times 10^{-4})$

$W = 3.5 \times 10^{-2} \times 8\pi \times 3 \times 10^{-4}$

$W = 84\pi \times 10^{-6}$

Using $\pi = \dfrac{22}{7}$:

$W = 84 \times \dfrac{22}{7} \times 10^{-6} = 264 \times 10^{-6}$ J

Comparing with $\alpha \times 10^{-6}$ J, we get $\alpha = 264$

Answer: $264$

subject: Physics
q: A hydraulic press containing water has two arms with diameters as mentioned in the figure. A force of $10 \mathrm{~N}$ is applied on the surface of water in the thinner arm. The force required to be applied on the surface of water in the thicker arm to maintain equilibrium of water is _____N.
question: A hydraulic press containing water has two arms with diameters as mentioned in the figure. A force of $10 \mathrm{~N}$ is applied on the surface of water in the thinner arm. The force required to be applied on the surface of water in the thicker arm to maintain equilibrium of water is _____N.
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \frac{F_1}{A_1}=\frac{F_2}{A_2} \\ & \frac{F_1}{\pi(7)^2}=\frac{10}{\pi \times(0.7)^2} \\ & F_1=1000 \mathrm{~N}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: Surface tension of two liquids (having same densities), $T_{1}$ and $T_{2}$, are measured using capillary rise method utilizing two tubes with inner radii of $r_{1}$ and $r_{2}$ where $r_{1}>r_{2}$. The measured liquid heights in these tubes are $h_{1}$ and $h_{2}$ respectively. [Ignore the weight of the liquid about the lowest point of miniscus]. The heights $h_{1}$ and $h_{2}$ and surfaces tensions $T_{1}$ and $T_{2}$ satisfy the relation:
question: Surface tension of two liquids (having same densities), $T_{1}$ and $T_{2}$, are measured using capillary rise method utilizing two tubes with inner radii of $r_{1}$ and $r_{2}$ where $r_{1}>r_{2}$. The measured liquid heights in these tubes are $h_{1}$ and $h_{2}$ respectively. [Ignore the weight of the liquid about the lowest point of miniscus]. The heights $h_{1}$ and $h_{2}$ and surfaces tensions $T_{1}$ and $T_{2}$ satisfy the relation:
solution:
For capillary rise, the height of liquid in a tube is given by: $h = \frac{2T\cos\theta}{\rho g r}$, where $T$ is surface tension and $r$ is the tube radius.

For two liquids with equal density and contact angles:
$h_1 = \frac{2T_1\cos\theta}{\rho g r_1}$ and $h_2 = \frac{2T_2\cos\theta}{\rho g r_2}$

Taking the ratio: $\frac{h_1}{h_2} = \frac{T_1}{T_2} \times \frac{r_2}{r_1}$

Since $r_1 > r_2$, we have $\frac{r_2}{r_1} < 1$. The capillary rise is inversely proportional to tube radius, so the smaller radius tube will have greater height. Since the liquids are measured to have heights $h_1$ and $h_2$ with $r_1 > r_2$, and they have the same density, the empirical relation shows that both liquids rise to different heights inversely proportional to their radii. For liquids with the same density measured in different tubes, if the heights and radii satisfy the capillary rise equation, the surface tensions must be equal: $T_1 = T_2$, and $h_1 < h_2$ since $r_1 > r_2$.

subject: Physics
q: If the terminal speed of a sphere of gold (density $=19.5 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^3$ ) is $0.2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ in a viscous liquid (density $=1.5 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^3$ ) of the same size in the same liquid.
question: If the terminal speed of a sphere of gold (density $=19.5 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^3$ ) is $0.2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ in a viscous liquid (density $=1.5 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^3$ ) of the same size in the same liquid.
solution:
$\frac{\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{s}}}{\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{g}}}=\frac{\left(\rho_{\mathrm{s}}-\rho_{\ell}\right)}{\left(\rho_{\mathrm{g}}-\rho_{\ell}\right)}$
$\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{s}}=0.1 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$

subject: Physics
q: The excess pressure inside a soap bubble A in air is half the excess pressure inside another soap bubble B in air. If the volume of the bubble A is $n$ times the volume of the bubble $B$, then, the value of $n$ is ___.
question: The excess pressure inside a soap bubble A in air is half the excess pressure inside another soap bubble B in air. If the volume of the bubble A is $n$ times the volume of the bubble $B$, then, the value of $n$ is ___.
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \Delta \mathrm{P}=\frac{4 \mathrm{~T}}{\mathrm{R}} \\ & \frac{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{B}}}=\frac{\Delta \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{B}}}{\Delta \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{A}}}=2 \\ & \frac{\mathrm{~V}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{B}}}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{B}}}\right)^3=8\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: Two water drops each of radius 'r' coalesce to from a bigger drop. If ' T ' is the surface tension, the surface energy released in this process is :
question: Two water drops each of radius 'r' coalesce to from a bigger drop. If ' T ' is the surface tension, the surface energy released in this process is :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & 2 \times \frac{4}{3} \pi \mathrm{R}^3=\frac{4}{3} \pi \mathrm{r}^3 \Rightarrow \mathrm{r}=2^{1 / 3} \mathrm{R} \\ & \mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{i}}=2 \times 4 \pi \mathrm{R}^2 \mathrm{~T} \\ & \mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{f}}=4 \pi \mathrm{r}^2 \mathrm{~T}=4 \pi \mathrm{R}^2 \mathrm{~T} 2^{2 / 3} \\ & \therefore \text { Heat lost }=\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{i}}-\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{f}}=4 \pi \mathrm{R}^2 \mathrm{~T}\left[2-2^{2 / 3}\right]\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: Consider a completely full cylindrical water tank of height 1.6 m and cross-sectional area $0.5 \mathrm{~m}^2$. It has a small hole in its side at a height 90 cm from the bottom. Assume, the cross-sectional area of the hole to be negligibly small as compared to that of the water tank. If a load 50 kg is applied at the top surface of the water in the tank then the velocity of the water coming out at the instant when the hole is opened is : $\left(\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2\right)$
question: Consider a completely full cylindrical water tank of height 1.6 m and cross-sectional area $0.5 \mathrm{~m}^2$. It has a small hole in its side at a height 90 cm from the bottom. Assume, the cross-sectional area of the hole to be negligibly small as compared to that of the water tank. If a load 50 kg is applied at the top surface of the water in the tank then the velocity of the water coming out at the instant when the hole is opened is : $\left(\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2\right)$
solution:
Apply Bernouli equation between points $1 \& 2$
$\begin{aligned}
& P_1+\frac{1}{2} \rho v_1^2+\rho g h=P_2+\frac{1}{2} \rho v_2^2+0 \\ & P_0+\frac{\mathrm{mg}}{\mathrm{~A}}+\rho g \frac{70}{100}=P_0+\frac{1}{2} \rho v_2^2 \\ & \frac{5000}{0.5}+10^3 \times 10 \frac{70}{100}=\frac{1}{2} \times 10^3 v_2^2
\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned}
& 10^3+10^3 \times 7=\frac{10^3}{2} \mathrm{v}_2^2 \\ & \mathrm{v}_2^2=16 \\ & \mathrm{v}_2=4 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}
\end{aligned}$
As the tank area is large $\mathrm{v}_1$ is negligible compared to $\mathrm{v}_2$

subject: Physics
q: The diameter of an air bubble which was initially <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>, rises steadily through a solution of density $1750 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^3$ at the rate of $0.35 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}$. Coefficient of viscosity of the solution is ______ (Assume mass of the bubble to be negligible) (Answer in Poise to the nearest integer)
question: The diameter of an air bubble which was initially <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>, rises steadily through a solution of density $1750 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^3$ at the rate of $0.35 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}$. Coefficient of viscosity of the solution is ______ (Assume mass of the bubble to be negligible) (Answer in Poise to the nearest integer)
solution:
As the bubble is rising steadily the net force acting on it will be zero.

(Because of density of air is negligible, so the value of mg can be neglected)

So, the force of buoyancy B is equal to the weight of the displaced liquid acting upward is equal to the viscous force acting downward, i.e., B=F.

⇒4π3R3ρg=6πηRv

Putting R=1 mm=10-3 m, ρ=1.75×103 kg m-3, g=10 m s-2 and v=0.35×10-2 m s-1

η=109=1·11

In SI unit, η=11 poise CGS.

question: Water is flowing continuously from a tap having an internal diameter $8 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}$. The water velocity as it leaves the tap is $0.4 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$. The diameter of the water stream at a distance $2 \times 10^{-1} \mathrm{~m}$ below the lap is close to :

subject: Physics
q: Eight mercury drops, each of radius $r$, coalesce to form a bigger drop. The surface energy released in this process is _______. ($S$ is the surface tension of mercury).
question: Eight mercury drops, each of radius $r$, coalesce to form a bigger drop. The surface energy released in this process is _______. ($S$ is the surface tension of mercury).
solution:
Let the radius of the bigger drop be $R$. Since the total volume remains conserved during the process:

$8 \times \dfrac{4}{3} \pi r^3 = \dfrac{4}{3} \pi R^3$

$R^3 = 8r^3 \Rightarrow R = 2r$

Initial surface energy of the $8$ drops is:

$U_i = 8 \times (4 \pi r^2 S) = 32 \pi r^2 S$

Final surface energy of the bigger drop is:

$U_f = 4 \pi R^2 S = 4 \pi (2r)^2 S = 16 \pi r^2 S$

The surface energy released in the process is:

$\Delta U = U_i - U_f = 32 \pi r^2 S - 16 \pi r^2 S = 16 \pi r^2 S$

Answer: $16\pi r^2 S$

subject: Physics
q: A wooden block floating in a bucket of water has <math><mfrac><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>5</mn></mrow></mfrac></math> of its volume submerged. When certain amount of an oil is poured into the bucket, it is found that the block is just under the oil surface with half of its volume under water and half in oil. The density of oil relative to that of water is:
question: A wooden block floating in a bucket of water has <math><mfrac><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>5</mn></mrow></mfrac></math> of its volume submerged. When certain amount of an oil is poured into the bucket, it is found that the block is just under the oil surface with half of its volume under water and half in oil. The density of oil relative to that of water is:
solution:
Case-1, σ Vg=ρw45Vg …….(I)
Case-2, σ Vg=ρwV2g+ρoV2g …….(II)
From eq (I) & (II),
45ρw=ρw2+ρo2
45-12ρw=ρo2
8-510ρw=ρo2
ρoρw=35=0.6

subject: Physics
q: A metal block of base area 0.20 m2 is placed on a table, as shown in figure. A liquid film of thickness 0.25 mm is inserted between the block and the table. The block is pushed by a horizontal force of 0.1 N and moves with a constant speed. If the viscosity of the liquid is 5.0×10–3 Pl, the speed of block is ______ ×10-3 m s-1.
question: A metal block of base area 0.20 m2 is placed on a table, as shown in figure. A liquid film of thickness 0.25 mm is inserted between the block and the table. The block is pushed by a horizontal force of 0.1 N and moves with a constant speed. If the viscosity of the liquid is 5.0×10–3 Pl, the speed of block is ______ ×10-3 m s-1.
solution:
Viscous force is given by,

|F|=ηAΔvΔh

⇒0.1=5×10-3×0.2×v0.25×10-3

⇒v=0.025 m s-1

⇒v=25×10-3 m s-1

subject: Physics
q: Pressure inside two soap bubbles are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>01</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>02</mn></math> atmosphere, respectively. The ratio of their volumes is :
question: Pressure inside two soap bubbles are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>01</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>02</mn></math> atmosphere, respectively. The ratio of their volumes is :
solution:
Excess pressure inside a soap bubble is 4TR. Therefore,

P1-P0=4TR1=0.01 atm

P2-P0=4TR2=0.02 atm

Dividing,

12=R2R1

⇒R1=2R2

⇒V1V2=R13R23=8R23R23=81

subject: Physics
q: Two tubes of radii <math><msub><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> and lengths <math><msub><mrow><mi>l</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi>l</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2,</mn></mrow></msub></math> respectively, are connected in series and a liquid flows through each of them in stream line conditions. <math><msub><mrow><mi>P</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi>P</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> are pressure differences across the two tubes. If <math><msub><mrow><mi>P</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> is <math><mn>4</mn><msub><mrow><mi>P</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi>l</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> is <math> <mrow> <mfrac> <mrow> <msub> <mi>l</mi> <mn>1</mn> </msub> </mrow> <mrow> <mn>4</mn> </mrow> </mfrac> </mrow> </math> then the radius <math><msub><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> will be equal to :
question: Two tubes of radii <math><msub><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> and lengths <math><msub><mrow><mi>l</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi>l</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2,</mn></mrow></msub></math> respectively, are connected in series and a liquid flows through each of them in stream line conditions. <math><msub><mrow><mi>P</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi>P</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> are pressure differences across the two tubes. If <math><msub><mrow><mi>P</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> is <math><mn>4</mn><msub><mrow><mi>P</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi>l</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> is <math> <mrow> <mfrac> <mrow> <msub> <mi>l</mi> <mn>1</mn> </msub> </mrow> <mrow> <mn>4</mn> </mrow> </mfrac> </mrow> </math> then the radius <math><msub><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> will be equal to :
solution:
dVdt=π8pr4ηL

p1r14L1=p2r24L2

p1r14l1=4p1r24l14

⇒r24=r1416

⇒r2=r12

subject: Physics
q: In the diagram shown, the difference in the two tubes of the manometer is 5 cm, the cross-section of the tube at A and B is 6 mm2 and 10 mm2 respectively. The rate at which water flows through the tube is g=10 m s-2
question: In the diagram shown, the difference in the two tubes of the manometer is 5 cm, the cross-section of the tube at A and B is 6 mm2 and 10 mm2 respectively. The rate at which water flows through the tube is g=10 m s-2
solution:
6vA=10vB  ......1

12ρvA2-ρgh=12ρvB2  .........(2)

⇒vA=10.8 m s-1 

 dVdt=AvA

=6×10-2 cm2×1000.8 cm s-1=7.5 cc s-1

OR

By the concept of the venturi meter tube

rate of flow=Q=A1A22g(h1−h2)(A22−A12)  

=6×10×10−42×100×5(−36+100)×10−2

=7.5 cc s-1

subject: Physics
q: In a cylindrical water tank, there are two small holes $A$ and $B$ on the wall at a depth of $h_1$, from the surface of water and at a height of $h_2$ from the bottom of water tank. Surface of water is at height of $h_2$ from the bottom of water tank. Surface of water is at heigh $H$ from the bottom of water tank. Water coming out from both holes strikes the ground at the same point $S$. Find the ratio of $h_1$ and $h_2$
question: In a cylindrical water tank, there are two small holes $A$ and $B$ on the wall at a depth of $h_1$, from the surface of water and at a height of $h_2$ from the bottom of water tank. Surface of water is at height of $h_2$ from the bottom of water tank. Surface of water is at heigh $H$ from the bottom of water tank. Water coming out from both holes strikes the ground at the same point $S$. Find the ratio of $h_1$ and $h_2$
solution:
Range is same for both holes
$$
\therefore 2 \sqrt{\left(\mathrm{H}-h_1\right) h_1}=2 \sqrt{\left(\mathrm{H}-h_2\right) h_2}
$$
Squaring both sides,
$$
\begin{aligned}
& 4\left(H-h_1\right) \mathrm{h}_1=4\left(H-h_2\right) h_2 \\
& H h_1-h_1^2=H h_2-h_2^2
\end{aligned}
$$
On solving we get,
$$
H=h_1+h_2
$$
Hence, the ratio of $\frac{h_1}{h_2}$ depends on $H$.

subject: Physics
q:
A spherical ball of radius $1 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m}$ and density $10^5 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^3$ falls freely under gravity through a distance $h$ before entering a tank of water, If after entering in water the velocity of the ball does not change, then the value of $h$ is approximately:
(The coefficient of viscosity of water is $9.8 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~N} \mathrm{~s} / \mathrm{m}^2$ )
question:
A spherical ball of radius $1 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m}$ and density $10^5 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^3$ falls freely under gravity through a distance $h$ before entering a tank of water, If after entering in water the velocity of the ball does not change, then the value of $h$ is approximately:
(The coefficient of viscosity of water is $9.8 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~N} \mathrm{~s} / \mathrm{m}^2$ )
solution:
$\begin{aligned}& \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{T}}=\frac{2 \mathrm{~g}}{9} \frac{\mathrm{R}^2\left[\rho_{\mathrm{B}}-\rho_{\mathrm{L}}\right]}{\eta} \\& \Rightarrow \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{T}}=\frac{2}{9} \times \frac{10 \times\left(10^{-4}\right)^2}{9.8 \times 10^{-6}}\left[10^5-10^3\right] \\& \Rightarrow \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{T}}=224.5\end{aligned}$
when ball fall from height (h)
$\begin{aligned}& \mathrm{V}=\sqrt{2 \mathrm{gh}} \\
& \mathrm{h}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{V}^2}{2 \mathrm{~g}}\right)=2518 \mathrm{~m}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A liquid column of height $0.04 \mathrm{~cm}$ balances excess pressure of a soap bubble of certain radius. If density of liquid is $8 \times 10^3 \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~m}^{-3}$ and surface tension of soap solution is $0.28 \mathrm{Nm}^{-1}$, then diameter of the soap bubble is _____ $\mathrm{cm}$. (if $g=10 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-2}$ )
question: A liquid column of height $0.04 \mathrm{~cm}$ balances excess pressure of a soap bubble of certain radius. If density of liquid is $8 \times 10^3 \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~m}^{-3}$ and surface tension of soap solution is $0.28 \mathrm{Nm}^{-1}$, then diameter of the soap bubble is _____ $\mathrm{cm}$. (if $g=10 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-2}$ )
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \rho \mathrm{gh}=\frac{4 \mathrm{~S}}{\mathrm{R}} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{R}=\frac{4 \times 0.28}{8 \times 10^3 \times 10 \times 4 \times 10^{-4}} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{0.28}{8} \mathrm{~m}=\frac{28}{8} \mathrm{~cm} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{R}=3.5 \mathrm{~cm}\end{aligned}$
Diameter $=7 \mathrm{~cm}$

subject: Physics
q: A cylindrical vessel of $40$ cm radius is completely filled with water and its capacity is $528$ dm$^3$ (dm : decimeter). The vessel is placed on a solid block of exactly same height as vessel. If a small hole is made at $70$ cm below the top of water level, then horizontal range of water falling on the ground in the beginning is __________ cm.
question: A cylindrical vessel of $40$ cm radius is completely filled with water and its capacity is $528$ dm$^3$ (dm : decimeter). The vessel is placed on a solid block of exactly same height as vessel. If a small hole is made at $70$ cm below the top of water level, then horizontal range of water falling on the ground in the beginning is __________ cm.
solution:
Volume of the cylindrical vessel, $V = 528$ dm$^3 = 528000$ cm$^3$

Radius of the vessel, $r = 40$ cm

Let the height of the vessel be $H$.

$V = \pi r^2 H$

$528000 = \dfrac{22}{7} \times (40)^2 \times H$

$H = \dfrac{528000 \times 7}{22 \times 1600} = 105$ cm

The vessel is placed on a block of height equal to the vessel, so the height of the block is $105$ cm.

Depth of the hole from the top water level, $h = 70$ cm

Height of the hole from the ground, $y = \text{Height of block} + (H - h)$

$y = 105 + (105 - 70) = 140$ cm

Velocity of efflux, $v = \sqrt{2gh}$

Time of flight of water, $t = \sqrt{\dfrac{2y}{g}}$

Horizontal range, $R = v \times t = \sqrt{2gh} \times \sqrt{\dfrac{2y}{g}} = 2\sqrt{hy}$

$R = 2\sqrt{70 \times 140} = 2\sqrt{70 \times 70 \times 2} = 140\sqrt{2}$ cm

Answer: $140\sqrt{2}$

subject: Physics
q:
A capillary tube of radius 0.1 mm is partly dipped in water (surface tension $70 \mathrm{dyn} / \mathrm{cm}$ and glass water contact angle $\simeq 0^{\circ}$) with $30^{\circ}$ inclined with vertical. The length of water risen in the capillary is ________ cm.
$\left(\right.$ Take $\left.\mathrm{g}=9.8 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2\right)$
question:
A capillary tube of radius 0.1 mm is partly dipped in water (surface tension $70 \mathrm{dyn} / \mathrm{cm}$ and glass water contact angle $\simeq 0^{\circ}$) with $30^{\circ}$ inclined with vertical. The length of water risen in the capillary is ________ cm.
$\left(\right.$ Take $\left.\mathrm{g}=9.8 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2\right)$
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{h}=\frac{2 \mathrm{~T} \cos \theta}{\rho \mathrm{gr}}=\frac{2 \times 70 \times 1}{1 \times 980 \times 10^{-2}} \\ & \mathrm{~h}=\frac{100}{7} \mathrm{~cm} \\ & \sin 60^{\circ}=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\ell} \\ & \ell=\frac{\mathrm{h} \times 2}{\sqrt{3}} \\ & \ell=\frac{100}{7} \times \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \\ & =\frac{200}{7 \times \sqrt{3}} \\ & =16.49 \mathrm{~cm}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: There is a circular tube in a vertical plane. Two liquids which do not mix and of densities d1 and d2 are filled in the tube. Each liquid subtends 90o angle at centre. Radius joining their interface makes an angle α  with vertical. Ratio d 1 d 2   is :
question: There is a circular tube in a vertical plane. Two liquids which do not mix and of densities d1 and d2 are filled in the tube. Each liquid subtends 90o angle at centre. Radius joining their interface makes an angle α  with vertical. Ratio d 1 d 2   is :
solution:
Pressure at common interface of two liquid columns is same.



d 1 gR 1 - sin α = d 1 gR 1 - cos α + d 2 gR sin α + cos α

d 1 cos α - sin α = d 2 sin α + cos α

∴    d 1 d 2 = sin α + cos α cos α - sin α = tan α + 1 1 - tan α

subject: Physics
q: A jar filled with two non mixing liquids 1 and 2 having densities $\rho_1$ and $\rho_2$ respectively. A solid ball, made of a material of density $\rho_3$, is dropped in the jar. It comes to equilibrium in the position shown in the figure. Which of the following is true for $\rho_1, \rho_2$ and $\rho_3$ ?
question: A jar filled with two non mixing liquids 1 and 2 having densities $\rho_1$ and $\rho_2$ respectively. A solid ball, made of a material of density $\rho_3$, is dropped in the jar. It comes to equilibrium in the position shown in the figure. Which of the following is true for $\rho_1, \rho_2$ and $\rho_3$ ?
solution:
As liquid 1 floats above liquid 2 ,
$$
\rho_1 < \rho_2
$$
The ball is unable to sink into liquid 2 ,
$\rho_3 < \rho_2$ The ball is unable to rise over liquid 1 ,
$\begin{array}{ll} & \rho_1 < \rho_3 \\ \text { Thus, } & \rho_1 < \rho_3 < \rho_2\end{array}$

subject: Physics
q: An air bubble of radius $0.1 \mathrm{~cm}$ is in a liquid having surface tension $0.06 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}$ and density $10^3 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^3$. The pressure inside the bubble is $1100 \mathrm{Nm}^{-2}$ greater than the atmospheric pressure. At what depth is the bubble below the surface of the liquid? $\left(\mathrm{g}=9.8 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}\right)$
question: An air bubble of radius $0.1 \mathrm{~cm}$ is in a liquid having surface tension $0.06 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}$ and density $10^3 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^3$. The pressure inside the bubble is $1100 \mathrm{Nm}^{-2}$ greater than the atmospheric pressure. At what depth is the bubble below the surface of the liquid? $\left(\mathrm{g}=9.8 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}\right)$
solution:
Given: Radius of air bubble,
$$
r=0.1 \mathrm{~cm}=10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}
$$
Surface tension of liquid,
$$
S=0.06 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}=6 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}
$$
Density of liquid, $\rho=10^3 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^3$
Excess pressure inside the bubble,
$$
\rho_{\text {exe }}=1100 \mathrm{Nm}^{-2}
$$
Depth of bubble below the liquid surface, $h=$ ?
As we know,
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\rho_{\text {Excess }}=h \rho g+\frac{2 s}{r} \\
&\Rightarrow \quad 1100=h \times 10^3 \times 9.8+\frac{2 \times 6 \times 10^{-2}}{10^{-3}} \\
&\Rightarrow \quad 1100=9800 h+120 \\
&\Rightarrow \quad 9800 h=1100-120 \\
&\Rightarrow \quad h=\frac{980}{9800}=0.1 \mathrm{~m}
\end{aligned}
$$

q: If <math><mi mathvariant="normal">'</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">'</mi></math> is the mass of water that rises in a capillary tube of radius <math><mi mathvariant="normal">'</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">'</mi></math> , then mass of water which will rise in a capillary tube of radius <math><mi mathvariant="normal">'</mi><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">'</mi></math> is:

solution:
Mass of liquid rising in tube =ρAh
m=ρπr22Tcos⁡θρgr
m∝r
Mass of liquid rise in tube will become 2M

subject: Physics
q:
Consider following statements:
A. Surface tension arises due to extra energy of the molecules at the interior as compared to the molecules at the surface, of a liquid.
B. As the temperature of liquid rises, the coefficient of viscosity increases.
C. As the temperature of gas increases, the coefficient of viscosity increases
D. The onset of turbulence is determined by Reynold's number.
E. In a steady flow two stream lines never intersect.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Consider following statements:
A. Surface tension arises due to extra energy of the molecules at the interior as compared to the molecules at the surface, of a liquid.
B. As the temperature of liquid rises, the coefficient of viscosity increases.
C. As the temperature of gas increases, the coefficient of viscosity increases
D. The onset of turbulence is determined by Reynold's number.
E. In a steady flow two stream lines never intersect.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution: Surface tension arises due to extra energy of the molecules at the molecules at the surface as compared at the interior of a liquid. The coefficient of viscosity for a liquid decreases with rise in temperature where as it increases for gases with increase in temperature. The flow is turbulent for a Reynold's number greater than 2000. Stream lines never intersect in a steady flow.

subject: Physics
q: Wax is coated on the inner wall of a capillary tube and the tube is then dipped in water. Then, compared to the unwaxed capillary, the angle of contact $\theta$ and the height $h$ upto which water rises change. These changes are :
question: Wax is coated on the inner wall of a capillary tube and the tube is then dipped in water. Then, compared to the unwaxed capillary, the angle of contact $\theta$ and the height $h$ upto which water rises change. These changes are :
solution:
Angle of $\operatorname{contact} \theta$
$$
\cos \theta=\frac{\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{SA}}-\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{SL}}}{\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{LA}}}
$$
when water is on a waxy or oily surface
$\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{SA}} < \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{SL}} \cos \theta$ is negative i.e.,
$$
90^{\circ} < \theta < 180^{\circ}
$$
i.e., angle of contact $\theta$ increases
And for $\theta>90^{\circ}$ liquid level in capillary
tube fall. i.e., h decreases

q:
A cubical block of side 0.5 m floats on water with 30% of its volume under water. What is the maximum weight that can be put on the block without fully submerging it under water?

[Take, density of water  =103 kg/m3 ]
question:
A cubical block of side 0.5 m floats on water with 30% of its volume under water. What is the maximum weight that can be put on the block without fully submerging it under water?

[Take, density of water  =103 kg/m3 ]
solution:
In first case, when 30% of the cube inside water,
ρwVin g=ρcVg
VinV=ρcρw=0.3
Additional weight we can put w is,
Vρwg=ρcVg+w
w=Vρw-ρcg
=0.53×0.7×103×g
=87.5g N
Addition of mass that we can put =87.5 kg

subject: Physics
q: A capillary tube is immersed vertically in water and the height of the water column is <math> <mrow> <mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi>x</mi><mo>⁡</mo> </mrow> </math>. When this arrangement is taken into a mine of depth d, the height of the water column is <math> <mrow> <mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi>y</mi><mo>⁡</mo> </mrow> </math>. If R is the radius of earth, the ratio <math> <mrow> <mfrac> <mrow> <mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi>x</mi><mo>⁡</mo> </mrow> <mrow> <mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi>y</mi><mo>⁡</mo> </mrow> </mfrac> </mrow> </math> is :
question: A capillary tube is immersed vertically in water and the height of the water column is <math> <mrow> <mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi>x</mi><mo>⁡</mo> </mrow> </math>. When this arrangement is taken into a mine of depth d, the height of the water column is <math> <mrow> <mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi>y</mi><mo>⁡</mo> </mrow> </math>. If R is the radius of earth, the ratio <math> <mrow> <mfrac> <mrow> <mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi>x</mi><mo>⁡</mo> </mrow> <mrow> <mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi>y</mi><mo>⁡</mo> </mrow> </mfrac> </mrow> </math> is :
solution:
We know that

h = 2 T cos θ ρ gr

∴    x = 2 T cos θ ρ gr

Now, value of geff  at depth 'd' is

g eff = g 1 - d R

∴    y = 2 T cos θ ρ g eff · r

∴    x y = g eff g = 1 - d R

subject: Physics
q: Water flows into a large tank with flat bottom at the rate of <math><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mi> </mi><msup><mrow><mi>m</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mrow><mi>s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> Water is also leaking out of a hole of area <math><mn>1</mn><mi> </mi><mi>c</mi><msup><mrow><mi>m</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> at its bottom. If the height of the water in the tank remains steady then this height is:
question: Water flows into a large tank with flat bottom at the rate of <math><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mi> </mi><msup><mrow><mi>m</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mrow><mi>s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> Water is also leaking out of a hole of area <math><mn>1</mn><mi> </mi><mi>c</mi><msup><mrow><mi>m</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> at its bottom. If the height of the water in the tank remains steady then this height is:
solution:
Amount of water going out = amount of water coming in.

2gh×A=V

h=10-422×9.8×10-42 =5.1 cm

subject: Physics
q: A small ball of mass $m$ and density $\rho$ is dropped in a viscous liquid of density $\rho_0$. After sometime, the ball falls with constant velocity. The viscous force on the ball is :
question: A small ball of mass $m$ and density $\rho$ is dropped in a viscous liquid of density $\rho_0$. After sometime, the ball falls with constant velocity. The viscous force on the ball is :
solution:
$\mathrm{mg}-\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{B}}-\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{v}}=\mathrm{ma}$
$\mathrm{a}=0$ for constant velocity
$\begin{aligned}
& m g-F_B=F_v \\
& F_v=m g-v \rho_0 g=m g-\frac{m}{\rho} \rho_0 g=m g\left(1-\frac{\rho_0}{\rho}\right)
\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: Glycerin of density 1.25×103 kg m–3 is flowing through the conical section of pipe. The area of cross-section of the pipe at its ends are 10 cm2 and 5 cm2 and pressure drop across its length is 3 N m–2. The rate of flow of glycerine through the pipe is x×10–5 m3 s–1. The value of x is _____.
question: Glycerin of density 1.25×103 kg m–3 is flowing through the conical section of pipe. The area of cross-section of the pipe at its ends are 10 cm2 and 5 cm2 and pressure drop across its length is 3 N m–2. The rate of flow of glycerine through the pipe is x×10–5 m3 s–1. The value of x is _____.
solution:
It is given that

A1=10 cm2; A2=5 cm2

∆P=3 N m-2

By continuity equation,

A1v1=A2v2⇒v1=A2v2A1   ...(i)

By Bernoulli's equation,

12ρv22-12ρv12=∆P   ...(ii)

Using equation (i) in (ii)

∆P=ρ2(v22-A2v2A12)⇒∆P=ρv2221-A2A12⇒3=1.25×103×v2221-14⇒v22=6×41.25×103×3⇒v2=8×10-2 m s-1

So, the discharge rate is,

A2v2=x×10-5⇒x=5×10-4×8×10-210-5=4
